attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of persistent productive cough with purulent sputum. She experiences episodes of pleuritic chest pain during exacerbations, which occur every few weeks. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. She has a 20 pack-year smoking history but quit five years ago.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. She has clubbing of her fingers and bilateral wheeze on auscultation. Auscultation also reveals coarse crackles at both lung bases. Pulmonary function tests show FEV1 2.4 L (85% predicted), FVC 3.0 L (90% predicted), and FEV1/FVC ratio 0.80. High-resolution computed tomography of the chest shows no bronchial dilatation or bronchial wall thickening.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following most strongly suggests that bronchiectasis is the incorrect diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Bronchiectasis is defined as permanent and abnormal dilatation of the bronchi, typically diagnosed when the bronchoarterial ratio exceeds 1.0 on high-resolution computed tomography (HRCT). This patient presents with chronic productive cough and recurrent exacerbations, clinical features that could suggest bronchiectasis. However, the key to diagnosis lies in the imaging findings.

Absent bronchial dilatation on imaging effectively rules out bronchiectasis. High-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) is the gold standard for diagnosing bronchiectasis. The diagnosis requires demonstration of bronchial dilatation, typically defined as a bronchial diameter exceeding that of the adjacent vessel. Without these characteristic radiological changes on HRCT, bronchiectasis cannot be diagnosed, regardless of the clinical presentation. This patient likely has chronic suppurative lung disease (CSLD), which presents with similar clinical features but lacks the radiological evidence of bronchial dilatation. CSLD may represent a precursor to bronchiectasis and requires similar management approaches including chest physiotherapy, sputum clearance techniques, prompt treatment of exacerbations, and appropriate vaccinations.

History of pleuritic chest pain is a recognised feature during bronchiectasis exacerbations and does not suggest an alternative diagnosis. Many patients experience chest pain during acute exacerbations due to inflammation and increased coughing.

Normal spirometry is found in 20-30 per cent of patients with bronchiectasis, with a similar proportion demonstrating airflow obstruction. Therefore, normal pulmonary function tests do not exclude bronchiectasis. The diagnosis relies on imaging rather than spirometry findings.

Clubbing occurs in fewer than five per cent of patients with bronchiectasis but can be present, particularly in those with severe or longstanding disease. Its presence does not exclude the diagnosis.

Bilateral wheeze is an underrecognised feature of bronchiectasis, occurring in 20-30 per cent of patients. This is due to airway inflammation and bronchial hyperresponsiveness that can accompany the condition. The presence of wheeze does not rule out bronchiectasis and may actually support the diagnosis in the appropriate clinical context.

The critical point is that HRCT is essential for diagnosing bronchiectasis, and the absence of characteristic bronchial dilatation on this imaging modality definitively excludes the diagnosis. Patients with CSLD without bronchial dilatation should still receive comprehensive respiratory care similar to bronchiectasis management, as they may progress to develop structural changes over time.
learningPoints: High-resolution computed tomography is the gold standard for diagnosis of bronchiectasis, showing characteristic bronchial dilatation with bronchoarterial ratio >1.0, and absence of bronchial dilatation on HRCT effectively excludes the diagnosis.
Chronic suppurative lung disease without bronchial dilatation may be a precursor to bronchiectasis and requires similar management strategies.
Pleuritic chest pain, normal spirometry, clubbing and wheeze can all occur in bronchiectasis and do not exclude the diagnosis.
references: Chang AB, Bell SC, Byrnes CA, Dawkins P, Holland AE, Kennedy E, et al. Thoracic Society of Australia and New Zealand (TSANZ) position statement on chronic suppurative lung disease and bronchiectasis in children, adolescents and adults in Australia and New Zealand. Respirol Carlton Vic. 2023

Kim SH, Yang B, Yoo JY, Cho JY, Kang H, Shin YM, Kim EG, Lee KM, Choe KH. Clinical characteristics, radiological features, and disease severity of bronchiectasis according to the spirometric pattern. Scientific Reports. 2022 Aug 1;12(1):13167. Available at: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-022-17085-3
